name: Yttrium
name_origin: Named after the Swedish village of Ytterby, near where the mineral ytterbite (later renamed gadolinite) containing yttrium was discovered in 1787.

discovery.discoverer: Johan Gadolin (identified yttria); isolated by Friedrich Wohler in 1828

occurrence.abundance_summary: Yttrium is the 28th most abundant element in Earth's crust at about 33 ppm. Although classified as a transition metal, it is chemically grouped with the rare earths and occurs in nearly all rare-earth minerals such as monazite, xenotime, and bastnaesite.

compounds.name: Yttrium oxide (yttria)
compounds.summary: A white solid used as a host material for europium phosphors in red phosphors and in yttrium-stabilized zirconia.

compounds.name: Yttrium barium copper oxide (YBCO)
compounds.summary: A high-temperature superconductor that operates above the boiling point of liquid nitrogen.

compounds.name: Yttrium aluminum garnet (YAG)
compounds.summary: A synthetic crystal used as a host for laser dopants such as Nd:YAG and as a gemstone simulant.

compounds.name: Yttrium fluoride
compounds.summary: Used in optical coatings and as a precursor for high-purity yttrium metal.

compounds.name: Yttrium chloride
compounds.summary: A water-soluble salt used as a starting material in yttrium chemistry and catalysis.

uses: Red phosphors in color cathode-ray tubes and LEDs (Y2O3:Eu)
Nd:YAG and Er:YAG laser host crystals
Yttria-stabilized zirconia for thermal-barrier coatings and dental ceramics
Alloying agent to improve the strength of magnesium and aluminum alloys
reactivity_summary: Yttrium is a silvery, moderately reactive transition metal. Bulk yttrium is relatively stable in air due to a passivating oxide layer, but turnings or powder can ignite. It reacts slowly with water and dissolves readily in dilute acids. At high temperatures it reacts with most non-metals to form a wide range of compounds.
isotopes_summary: Yttrium has only one stable, naturally occurring isotope, 89Y (100% natural abundance), making it a mononuclidic element. More than 30 synthetic radioisotopes are known; 90Y (half-life 64 hours) is widely used in medicine for radiation therapy, including treatment of liver cancer and lymphoma.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Yttrium
